metadata.title: PFAS 'forever chemicals' — 3M/DuPont/Chemours, the AFFF water settlements, the decades of suppressed knowledge, the chipmaking dependency, and the 2025-26 EPA rollback
metadata.status: settlements, the suppression history, the chipmaking use, and the EPA rule changes are FACT (DOJ/SEC/court/EPA/Annals of Global Health study); the 'harm vs litigation-economy vs industrial-essentiality' tensions are graded separately; dignified framing for affected communities
metadata.frame: A mass-tort + environmental + supply-chain case that uniquely BRIDGES the corpus's chip block: the same PFAS that contaminate drinking water are 'essential' to semiconductor fabrication. Five strands: (1) the MAKERS (3M, DuPont and its spinoffs Chemours/Corteva); (2) the WATER SETTLEMENTS (3M up to $10.3B + Chemours/DuPont/Corteva $1.185B via the AFFF MDL); (3) the SUPPRESSION — 3M/DuPont hid PFAS hazard evidence since the 1960s (the verification/cover-up theme, rhyming with tobacco + the reproducibility crisis); (4) the CHIPMAKING dependency (1000+ uses; SEMI calls them un-removable; no federal discharge limits) — the cross-link to spec-semiconductor-logistics-standards; (5) the 2025-26 EPA ROLLBACK (keep PFOA/PFOS but extend to 2031, rescind GenX/PFHxS/PFNA/PFBS limits) — the same enforcement-reversal pattern as the crypto/spyware/Adani files. Ties to the mass-tort cluster (Camp Lejeune/opioids/asbestos), Semiconductor_Materials/SEMI, EPA.

the_makers: PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) are a class of ~15,000 'forever chemicals' (they don't break down). The dominant makers: 3M (the original) and DuPont — which spun its PFAS/materials liabilities into Chemours (2015) and split again into Corteva (agriculture). This corporate restructuring (spinning liabilities into a separate entity) echoes the liability-engineering playbook (cf. J&J Texas two-step / opioid bankruptcy). grade: fact.
the_water_settlements: Via the AFFF (aqueous film-forming foam) MDL 2873 (US District Court, Charleston SC): 3M agreed to up to $10.3B present value over 13 years to public water suppliers (final approval Mar 29 2024; e.g., NH received a ~$8M first payment Sept 2025). Chemours, DuPont, and Corteva contributed a combined $1.185B (Chemours ~$592M / DuPont ~$400M / Corteva ~$193M, June 2023), plus a 2025 New Jersey comprehensive environmental settlement. These resolve PUBLIC-WATER-SYSTEM claims; personal-injury/property claims continue. grade: fact.
the_suppression: The verification/cover-up core (peer-reviewed): an Annals of Global Health study found 3M and DuPont actively SUPPRESSED evidence that PFAS were hazardous from the 1960s. DuPont's lab knew C-8 (PFOA) was 'highly toxic when inhaled' by 1970; a 1979 study had dogs die after one dose; in 1980 the companies learned of birth defects among workers' children and HID it, with a 1981 internal memo denying any birth-defect link. ProPublica documented '3M convincing a scientist the PFAS she found in human blood were safe' (toxic gaslighting). This is the same suppress-the-evidence pattern as Big Tobacco and the verification-collapse theme. grade: fact (documented internal records / the study).
the_chipmaking_dependency: The cross-link to the chip block: the semiconductor industry has identified 1000+ uses of PFAS in manufacturing (photoresists, rinses, etchants, coolants, seals) for their inertness/purity/thermal stability. SEMI argues PFAS are 'essential' and that eliminating low-volume-exemption PFAS 'would result in a complete shutdown of all US domestic semiconductor manufacturing'; there are currently NO federal Clean Water Act effluent limits on chipmaking PFAS discharges. IMEC is researching PFAS-free resists/rinses. So the CHIPS-Act reshoring push and the PFAS-cleanup push are in direct tension (cf. Building Chips in America Act). grade: fact (the uses, the no-limit status); the 'shutdown' claim is an industry assertion (contested).
the_epa_rollback: Another enforcement-reversal: EPA's Apr-10-2024 final drinking-water rule (NPDWR) set limits for 6 PFAS (PFOA/PFOS at 4 ppt). In 2025-26 the EPA proposed to KEEP PFOA/PFOS but EXTEND compliance to 2031, and to RESCIND the limits for PFHxS, PFNA, GenX (HFPO-DA), and the PFBS hazard-index mixture (proposed May 18 2026; comments to Jul 20 2026). The protective rule is being partially unwound on a political clock — the same prosecuted/regulated-then-reversed pattern the corpus tracks across crypto, spyware, and Adani. grade: fact.
the_honest_reading: PFAS is the mass-tort that is ALSO a present-tense industrial dependency: documented decades of concealed harm (3M/DuPont) and multibillion water settlements on one side; genuine 'essentiality' to chip fabrication (and firefighting foam, medical devices) on the other; and a 2025-26 regulatory rollback unwinding part of the protection. The liability was spun into separate entities (Chemours/Corteva); the cleanup cost lands on water utilities and their ratepayers; and the chip-reshoring agenda collides with the cleanup agenda. Both true: real, suppressed, adjudicated harm AND a chemical the modern economy is genuinely hooked on. Dignified framing for contaminated communities. Overlay; excluded from the proofs.

sources.name: 3M — $10.3B public-water PFAS settlement (final approval Mar 2024, Charleston SC)
ProPublica — Toxic Gaslighting: how 3M buried the PFAS evidence
CBS — study: 3M/DuPont hid PFAS evidence (Annals of Global Health)
SEMI — PFAS in semiconductor manufacturing (industry 'essential' position)
EWG — chipmaking PFAS discharges have no federal limits (Building Chips in America Act)
White & Case — EPA partially rolls back PFAS drinking-water rule (2025-26)

edges.raw_instrument: spun off from DuPont to carry PFAS/materials liabilities (2015)
up to $10.3B / 13yr public-water settlement (final approval Mar 2024, Charleston SC)
contamination of public drinking-water systems nationwide
settlement funds routed to water suppliers for remediation (ratepayers bear residual cost)
Apr-2024 NPDWR (6 PFAS; PFOA/PFOS 4 ppt); 2025-26 partial rollback (keep PFOA/PFOS to 2031; rescind GenX/PFHxS/PFNA/PFBS)
1000+ chipmaking uses (resists/rinses/etchants/coolants); SEMI calls them 'essential'; no federal discharge limits
industry seeks exemptions; claims removing low-volume-exemption PFAS would shut down all US chipmaking (industry assertion)
suppressed PFAS hazard evidence from the 1960s ('toxic gaslighting'; ProPublica)
knew C-8 toxic by 1970; hid 1980 worker birth-defect cases; 1981 denial memo
decades of concealed-harm evidence (Annals of Global Health study) — the verification/cover-up theme
